chunk_summary: Guidance on optimizing latch, recognizing tongue-tie, and supporting milk supply for breastfeeding infants 0–6 months, emphasizing behavioral strategies, functional assessments, and workplace accommodations.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of latch optimization in effective breastfeeding and milk supply.
Recognize signs and functional implications of tongue-tie in infants.
Implement nonpharmacologic strategies to support milk production and maternal well-being.
Plan breastfeeding support for mothers returning to work, including expression and feeding schedules.
key_facts: Aim for 8–12 effective feeds per day in early infancy to optimize milk supply.
Persistent maternal nipple pain, clicking, poor weight gain, or latch issues may indicate ankyloglossia (tongue-tie).
Frenotomy can improve maternal pain and milk transfer if tongue-tie is functionally significant.
Routine top-ups without breast stimulation can worsen milk supply due to supply-demand imbalance.
Workplace lactation rooms and paid maternity benefits support sustained breastfeeding; otherwise, emphasize morning, evening, and night feeds.
reference_quote: Assess for ankyloglossia when there is persistent maternal nipple pain, clicking, poor weight gain, or inability to maintain latch despite good positioning. (Source: NICE guidance)
document_key_topics: Nonpharmacologic strategies for milk supply support
